instruction: Implement perfect forwarding in C++ with practical examples showing universal references, std::forward, and the common pitfalls including the forwarding reference vs rvalue reference distinction.
output:
<think>
Perfect forwarding is one of the most important but confusing parts of modern C++. Let me clarify the concepts.

Key distinction:
- `T&&` where T is a deduced template parameter = forwarding/universal reference (binds to both lvalues and rvalues)
- `SomeType&&` where SomeType is a concrete type = rvalue reference (only binds to rvalues)

Reference collapsing rules:
- T& & -> T&
- T& && -> T&
- T&& & -> T&
- T&& && -> T&&

When an lvalue is passed to `template<typename T> void f(T&& x)`, T deduces as `U&`, making the parameter `U& &&` which collapses to `U&`. When an rvalue is passed, T deduces as `U`, making the parameter `U&&`.

std::forward<T>(x) re-applies the value category:
- If T is `U&` (lvalue was passed), forward returns `U&` (lvalue)
- If T is `U` (rvalue was passed), forward returns `U&&` (rvalue)

Common pitfalls:
1. Using && on a non-template parameter (not a forwarding reference)
2. Forwarding the same argument multiple times (moved-from state on second use)
3. auto&& in range-for (actually IS a forwarding reference)
4. Deduction guides with forwarding references in constructors

I'll show:
- Factory function (make_unique-like) using forwarding
- Emplace pattern for containers
- The "forward once" rule
- SFINAE interaction
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
#include <vector>
#include <memory>
#include <utility>
#include <type_traits>

// ===== Helper to show value categories =====

template <typename T>
constexpr const char* value_category() {
    if constexpr (std::is_lvalue_reference_v<T>) return "lvalue";
    else if constexpr (std::is_rvalue_reference_v<T>) return "rvalue";
    else return "prvalue";
}

// ===== Basic perfect forwarding =====

class Widget {
public:
    std::string name;
    std::vector<int> data;

    Widget(std::string n, std::vector<int> d)
        : name(std::move(n)), data(std::move(d)) {
        std::cout << "  Widget(" << name << ") constructed\n";
    }

    Widget(const Widget& w) : name(w.name), data(w.data) {
        std::cout << "  Widget(" << name << ") COPIED\n";
    }

    Widget(Widget&& w) noexcept : name(std::move(w.name)), data(std::move(w.data)) {
        std::cout << "  Widget(" << name << ") MOVED\n";
    }
};

// Factory with perfect forwarding — like make_unique
template <typename T, typename... Args>
std::unique_ptr<T> make(Args&&... args) {
    // std::forward preserves the value category of each argument
    return std::make_unique<T>(std::forward<Args>(args)...);
}

// ===== Forwarding reference vs rvalue reference =====

// THIS is a forwarding reference (T is deduced)
template <typename T>
void forwarding_ref(T&& x) {
    std::cout << "  forwarding_ref: T=" << value_category<T&&>() << "\n";
}

// THIS is NOT a forwarding reference (string is concrete)
void rvalue_ref(std::string&& x) {
    std::cout << "  rvalue_ref: got rvalue\n";
}

// NOT a forwarding reference (vector<T> is not the deduced type)
template <typename T>
void not_forwarding(std::vector<T>&& x) {
    std::cout << "  not_forwarding: only accepts rvalue vectors\n";
}

// ===== Emplace pattern with forwarding =====

template <typename Container, typename... Args>
void emplace_log(Container& c, Args&&... args) {
    std::cout << "  Emplacing with " << sizeof...(Args) << " args\n";
    c.emplace_back(std::forward<Args>(args)...);
}

// ===== PITFALL: Forwarding the same argument twice =====

template <typename T>
void forward_twice_bad(T&& x) {
    // BAD: After first forward, x may be moved-from
    // auto a = std::forward<T>(x);  // x potentially moved
    // auto b = std::forward<T>(x);  // UNDEFINED — moved-from state!
    std::cout << "  (Don't forward the same arg twice)\n";
}

template <typename T>
void forward_twice_safe(T&& x) {
    // GOOD: Copy first, then forward
    auto copy = x;  // always copies (x is an lvalue in this scope)
    auto forwarded = std::forward<T>(x);  // OK — first and only forward
    std::cout << "  Safe: copied then forwarded\n";
}

// ===== Constrained forwarding with SFINAE/concepts =====

template <typename T>
    requires std::is_constructible_v<std::string, T>
void set_name(T&& name) {
    std::string n = std::forward<T>(name);
    std::cout << "  Name set to: " << n << "\n";
}

// ===== auto&& as forwarding reference =====

void demo_auto_forwarding() {
    std::vector<std::string> v = {"hello", "world"};

    // auto&& in range-for IS a forwarding reference
    for (auto&& elem : v) {
        // elem is std::string& (lvalue ref to vector element)
        elem += "!";  // modifies in place
    }

    for (const auto& elem : v) {
        std::cout << "  " << elem << "\n";
    }
}

int main() {
    std::cout << "=== Perfect forwarding factory ===\n";
    std::string name = "Alice";
    auto w1 = make<Widget>(name, std::vector{1, 2, 3});           // copies name
    auto w2 = make<Widget>(std::move(name), std::vector{4, 5});   // moves name
    auto w3 = make<Widget>("Bob", std::vector{6});                 // constructs from literal

    std::cout << "\n=== Forwarding ref vs rvalue ref ===\n";
    std::string s = "test";
    forwarding_ref(s);            // T = string&, lvalue
    forwarding_ref(std::move(s)); // T = string, rvalue
    forwarding_ref("literal");    // T = const char(&)[8], lvalue

    // rvalue_ref(s);  // COMPILE ERROR — can't bind lvalue
    rvalue_ref(std::string("temp")); // OK

    std::vector<int> v = {1, 2};
    // not_forwarding(v);  // COMPILE ERROR — not a forwarding ref!
    not_forwarding(std::vector{1, 2}); // OK — rvalue

    std::cout << "\n=== Emplace with forwarding ===\n";
    std::vector<Widget> widgets;
    widgets.reserve(3);
    emplace_log(widgets, "Direct", std::vector{1, 2, 3});

    std::cout << "\n=== auto&& forwarding ===\n";
    demo_auto_forwarding();

    std::cout << "\n=== Constrained forwarding ===\n";
    set_name("literal");
    std::string n2 = "owned";
    set_name(n2);
    set_name(std::move(n2));

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement C++ fold expressions with practical examples showing parameter pack expansion, binary and unary folds, and how to use them for building type-safe variadic APIs.
output:
<think>
Fold expressions (C++17) simplify operations on parameter packs. Before C++17, you needed recursive template instantiation. Now you can fold directly.

Four forms:
1. Unary right fold: (pack op ...) => e1 op (e2 op (e3 op e4))
2. Unary left fold: (... op pack) => ((e1 op e2) op e3) op e4
3. Binary right fold: (pack op ... op init) => e1 op (e2 op (e3 op init))
4. Binary left fold: (init op ... op pack) => ((init op e1) op e2) op e3

The operator can be any binary operator including comma.

Practical uses:
1. Sum/product of variadic args: (args + ...)
2. All/any predicates: (args && ...) / (args || ...)
3. Print all args: ((std::cout << args << " "), ...)
4. Type-safe printf: fold over args with format checking
5. Builder pattern with chained operations
6. Tuple-like operations

I'll demonstrate:
- Basic arithmetic folds
- Logical folds for compile-time type checking
- Comma fold for side effects (printing, logging)
- Fold with custom operator overloading
- Practical: type-safe logging with fold
- Practical: compile-time string hash with fold
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <string>
#include <sstream>
#include <type_traits>
#include <tuple>
#include <functional>
#include <vector>

// ===== Basic fold expressions =====

// Sum: unary left fold with +
template <typename... Args>
auto sum(Args... args) {
    return (... + args);  // ((a + b) + c) + d
}

// Product: unary left fold with *
template <typename... Args>
auto product(Args... args) {
    return (... * args);
}

// All: unary left fold with &&
template <typename... Args>
bool all(Args... args) {
    return (... && args);
}

// Any: unary left fold with ||
template <typename... Args>
bool any(Args... args) {
    return (... || args);
}

// ===== Comma fold for side effects =====

// Print all arguments with separator
template <typename... Args>
void print(Args&&... args) {
    bool first = true;
    // Binary left fold with comma operator
    ((std::cout << (first ? (first = false, "") : ", ") << args), ...);
    std::cout << "\n";
}

// Push all elements into a vector
template <typename T, typename... Args>
void push_all(std::vector<T>& v, Args&&... args) {
    (v.push_back(std::forward<Args>(args)), ...);
}

// ===== Type checking with folds =====

// Check if all types are arithmetic
template <typename... Ts>
constexpr bool all_arithmetic = (... && std::is_arithmetic_v<Ts>);

// Constrained sum — only accepts arithmetic types
template <typename... Args>
auto safe_sum(Args... args) {
    static_assert(all_arithmetic<Args...>,
                  "safe_sum requires all arithmetic types");
    return (... + args);
}

// ===== Fold with custom operations =====

// Pipeline: fold function application
template <typename T, typename... Fns>
auto pipeline(T value, Fns... fns) {
    return (... , (value = fns(value), value));
    // Equivalent to: value = f1(value); value = f2(value); ...; return value;
}

// Cleaner version using a helper
template <typename T, typename Fn>
T apply(T val, Fn fn) { return fn(val); }

template <typename T, typename... Fns>
T pipe(T initial, Fns... fns) {
    return (apply(initial, fns), ...); // This doesn't chain correctly
    // Need binary fold:
}

// Correct pipeline using binary left fold with custom op
struct PipeOp {
    template <typename T, typename F>
    friend auto operator|(T&& val, F&& fn) {
        return fn(std::forward<T>(val));
    }
};

template <typename T, typename... Fns>
auto chain(T initial, Fns... fns) {
    // Binary left fold with |
    return (initial | ... | fns);
}

// ===== Fold-based string builder =====

template <typename... Args>
std::string concat(Args&&... args) {
    std::ostringstream oss;
    (oss << ... << args);  // Binary left fold with <<
    return oss.str();
}

// ===== Fold for index sequences =====

template <typename Tuple, typename Fn, std::size_t... Is>
void for_each_impl(Tuple&& t, Fn&& fn, std::index_sequence<Is...>) {
    (fn(std::get<Is>(t)), ...);  // Comma fold over indices
}

template <typename... Ts, typename Fn>
void for_each_tuple(const std::tuple<Ts...>& t, Fn&& fn) {
    for_each_impl(t, std::forward<Fn>(fn),
                  std::index_sequence_for<Ts...>{});
}

// ===== Compile-time fold: min/max =====

template <typename T>
constexpr T ct_min(T a, T b) { return a < b ? a : b; }

template <typename T, typename... Rest>
constexpr T min_of(T first, Rest... rest) {
    if constexpr (sizeof...(rest) == 0) {
        return first;
    } else {
        return ct_min(first, min_of(rest...));
    }
}

// With fold expression (using binary right fold):
template <typename T>
constexpr T min_fold_helper(T a, T b) { return a < b ? a : b; }

// Fold approach requires a wrapper since min isn't an operator
// But we can use the comma fold with a running minimum:
template <typename T, typename... Rest>
constexpr T minimum(T first, Rest... rest) {
    T result = first;
    ((result = result < rest ? result : rest), ...);
    return result;
}

int main() {
    std::cout << "=== Basic folds ===\n";
    std::cout << "sum(1,2,3,4,5) = " << sum(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) << "\n";
    std::cout << "product(1,2,3,4) = " << product(1, 2, 3, 4) << "\n";
    std::cout << "all(true,true,false) = " << all(true, true, false) << "\n";
    std::cout << "any(false,false,true) = " << any(false, false, true) << "\n";

    std::cout << "\n=== Print fold ===\n";
    print(1, "hello", 3.14, 'x');

    std::cout << "\n=== Vector push_all ===\n";
    std::vector<int> v;
    push_all(v, 10, 20, 30, 40);
    print(v[0], v[1], v[2], v[3]);

    std::cout << "\n=== String concat fold ===\n";
    std::cout << concat("Hello", ' ', "World", '!', " #", 42) << "\n";

    std::cout << "\n=== Pipeline fold ===\n";
    auto result = chain(
        5,
        [](int x) { return x * 2; },
        [](int x) { return x + 10; },
        [](int x) { return x * x; }
    );
    std::cout << "5 -> *2 -> +10 -> ^2 = " << result << "\n";

    std::cout << "\n=== Tuple for_each ===\n";
    auto t = std::make_tuple(1, "hello", 3.14);
    for_each_tuple(t, [](const auto& x) {
        std::cout << "  " << x << "\n";
    });

    std::cout << "\n=== Compile-time minimum ===\n";
    constexpr int m = minimum(5, 3, 7, 1, 4);
    std::cout << "min(5,3,7,1,4) = " << m << "\n";
    static_assert(m == 1);

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement C++ coroutines from scratch: create a Generator<T> type that yields values lazily, a Task<T> for async operations, and show the underlying promise_type machinery that makes co_yield and co_await work.
output:
<think>
C++20 coroutines are low-level machinery — the compiler generates the state machine, but we need to provide the types that tell the compiler what to do. The three key components:

1. Promise type: controls coroutine behavior (initial/final suspend, yield_value, return_value, etc.)
2. Coroutine handle: type-erased handle to the suspended coroutine
3. Return type: what the coroutine function returns (Generator<T>, Task<T>, etc.)

For Generator<T>:
- co_yield suspends the coroutine and makes a value available
- The caller resumes with next()/operator++ to get the next value
- promise_type::yield_value stores the value and suspends
- initial_suspend returns suspend_always (lazy start)
- final_suspend returns suspend_always (so we can detect completion)

For Task<T>:
- co_await suspends until an async operation completes
- co_return provides the final result
- promise_type::return_value stores the result
- get_return_object creates the Task
- The awaiter customizes suspend/resume behavior

The Generator is simpler and more commonly needed, so I'll focus more on that. For Task, I'll show the basic async machinery.

Key compiler requirements:
- Return type must have a nested `promise_type` (or specialize coroutine_traits)
- promise_type must have: get_return_object(), initial_suspend(), final_suspend(), unhandled_exception()
- For co_yield: yield_value()
- For co_return: return_value() or return_void()
</think>

```cpp
#include <coroutine>
#include <iostream>
#include <optional>
#include <stdexcept>
#include <string>
#include <vector>

// ===== Generator<T>: lazy sequence via co_yield =====

template <typename T>
class Generator {
public:
    // The promise_type is what the compiler looks for
    struct promise_type {
        T current_value;
        std::exception_ptr exception;

        Generator get_return_object() {
            return Generator{std::coroutine_handle<promise_type>::from_promise(*this)};
        }

        // Lazy: don't run until first next() call
        std::suspend_always initial_suspend() noexcept { return {}; }

        // Keep alive so we can check done()
        std::suspend_always final_suspend() noexcept { return {}; }

        // co_yield value — store and suspend
        std::suspend_always yield_value(T value) {
            current_value = std::move(value);
            return {};
        }

        void return_void() {}  // co_return; (no value)

        void unhandled_exception() {
            exception = std::current_exception();
        }
    };

    // Iterator for range-for support
    struct iterator {
        std::coroutine_handle<promise_type> handle;
        bool done;

        iterator& operator++() {
            handle.resume();
            done = handle.done();
            return *this;
        }

        T& operator*() { return handle.promise().current_value; }
        bool operator!=(const iterator& other) const { return done != other.done; }
    };

    iterator begin() {
        handle_.resume();
        return {handle_, handle_.done()};
    }

    iterator end() {
        return {handle_, true};
    }

    // Manual iteration
    std::optional<T> next() {
        if (handle_.done()) return std::nullopt;
        handle_.resume();
        if (handle_.done()) return std::nullopt;
        if (handle_.promise().exception)
            std::rethrow_exception(handle_.promise().exception);
        return handle_.promise().current_value;
    }

    Generator(Generator&& other) noexcept : handle_(other.handle_) {
        other.handle_ = nullptr;
    }

    ~Generator() {
        if (handle_) handle_.destroy();
    }

private:
    explicit Generator(std::coroutine_handle<promise_type> h) : handle_(h) {}
    std::coroutine_handle<promise_type> handle_;
};

// ===== Example generators =====

Generator<int> fibonacci() {
    int a = 0, b = 1;
    while (true) {
        co_yield a;
        auto next = a + b;
        a = b;
        b = next;
    }
}

Generator<int> range(int start, int end, int step = 1) {
    for (int i = start; i < end; i += step) {
        co_yield i;
    }
}

Generator<std::string> lines(const std::string& text) {
    size_t pos = 0;
    while (pos < text.size()) {
        auto nl = text.find('\n', pos);
        if (nl == std::string::npos) {
            co_yield text.substr(pos);
            break;
        }
        co_yield text.substr(pos, nl - pos);
        pos = nl + 1;
    }
}

// Chained generator: filter
Generator<int> filter_even(Generator<int> source) {
    for (auto val : source) {
        if (val % 2 == 0) {
            co_yield val;
        }
    }
}

// ===== Task<T>: simple async task =====

template <typename T>
class Task {
public:
    struct promise_type {
        T result;
        std::exception_ptr exception;

        Task get_return_object() {
            return Task{std::coroutine_handle<promise_type>::from_promise(*this)};
        }

        std::suspend_never initial_suspend() noexcept { return {}; } // eager
        std::suspend_always final_suspend() noexcept { return {}; }

        void return_value(T value) {
            result = std::move(value);
        }

        void unhandled_exception() {
            exception = std::current_exception();
        }
    };

    T get() {
        if (handle_.promise().exception)
            std::rethrow_exception(handle_.promise().exception);
        return std::move(handle_.promise().result);
    }

    bool done() const { return handle_.done(); }

    ~Task() { if (handle_) handle_.destroy(); }
    Task(Task&& other) noexcept : handle_(other.handle_) { other.handle_ = nullptr; }

private:
    explicit Task(std::coroutine_handle<promise_type> h) : handle_(h) {}
    std::coroutine_handle<promise_type> handle_;
};

// Example task
Task<int> compute_sum(int n) {
    int sum = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i <= n; ++i) sum += i;
    co_return sum;
}

int main() {
    std::cout << "=== Fibonacci Generator ===\n";
    auto fib = fibonacci();
    for (int i = 0; i < 10; ++i) {
        auto val = fib.next();
        if (val) std::cout << *val << " ";
    }
    std::cout << "\n";

    std::cout << "\n=== Range with range-for ===\n";
    for (int x : range(0, 10, 2)) {
        std::cout << x << " ";
    }
    std::cout << "\n";

    std::cout << "\n=== Chained generators ===\n";
    for (int x : filter_even(range(0, 20))) {
        std::cout << x << " ";
    }
    std::cout << "\n";

    std::cout << "\n=== Line generator ===\n";
    for (auto& line : lines("first\nsecond\nthird")) {
        std::cout << "  '" << line << "'\n";
    }

    std::cout << "\n=== Task ===\n";
    auto task = compute_sum(100);
    std::cout << "Sum 1..100 = " << task.get() << "\n";

    return 0;
}
```

instruction: Implement custom user-defined literals in C++ for building a type-safe unit system that converts between meters, kilometers, miles, and light-years at compile time with constexpr.
output:
<think>
User-defined literals (UDLs) allow writing expressions like `42_km` or `3.5_miles`. Combined with constexpr, all conversions happen at compile time — zero runtime cost.

Design:
1. A base Distance type templated on a tag (for type safety) that stores value in a canonical unit (meters)
2. UDL operators: operator""_m, operator""_km, operator""_mi, operator""_ly
3. Conversion functions between units
4. Arithmetic operators (+, -, *, /) that work with distances
5. Comparison operators
6. Everything constexpr for compile-time evaluation

The key insight: store everything internally as meters (the canonical unit). UDLs convert from the user's unit to meters on construction. Display functions convert back.

For type safety, I could use different types per unit, but that makes arithmetic between units verbose. Better approach: single Distance type that stores meters, with conversion on input/output. This is simpler and allows direct arithmetic between distances regardless of original unit.

For the UDL syntax:
- `long double` parameter for floating-point: `operator""_km(long double val)`
- `unsigned long long` parameter for integers: `operator""_km(unsigned long long val)`
- Must be in a namespace to avoid ODR issues

I'll also show consteval for values that MUST be compile-time, and static_assert to verify conversions.
</think>

```cpp
#include <iostream>
#include <cmath>
#include <compare>

// ===== Distance class: stores meters internally =====

class Distance {
    double meters_;

    constexpr explicit Distance(double meters) : meters_(meters) {}

public:
    // Named constructors
    static constexpr Distance from_meters(double m) { return Distance(m); }
    static constexpr Distance from_km(double km) { return Distance(km * 1000.0); }
    static constexpr Distance from_miles(double mi) { return Distance(mi * 1609.344); }
    static constexpr Distance from_light_years(double ly) { return Distance(ly * 9.461e15); }

    // Getters
    constexpr double meters() const { return meters_; }
    constexpr double kilometers() const { return meters_ / 1000.0; }
    constexpr double miles() const { return meters_ / 1609.344; }
    constexpr double light_years() const { return meters_ / 9.461e15; }

    // Arithmetic
    constexpr Distance operator+(Distance other) const {
        return Distance(meters_ + other.meters_);
    }
    constexpr Distance operator-(Distance other) const {
        return Distance(meters_ - other.meters_);
    }
    constexpr Distance operator*(double scalar) const {
        return Distance(meters_ * scalar);
    }
    constexpr Distance operator/(double scalar) const {
        return Distance(meters_ / scalar);
    }
    constexpr double operator/(Distance other) const {
        return meters_ / other.meters_;  // ratio
    }

    // Comparison
    constexpr auto operator<=>(const Distance& other) const = default;

    // Friend for scalar * distance
    friend constexpr Distance operator*(double scalar, Distance d) {
        return Distance(d.meters_ * scalar);
    }

    // Stream output
    friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& os, Distance d) {
        if (d.meters_ >= 9.461e15)
            return os << d.light_years() << " ly";
        if (d.meters_ >= 1000.0)
            return os << d.kilometers() << " km";
        if (d.meters_ >= 1.0)
            return os << d.meters() << " m";
        return os << d.meters() * 100.0 << " cm";
    }

    // UDL friends
    friend constexpr Distance operator""_m(long double val);
    friend constexpr Distance operator""_m(unsigned long long val);
    friend constexpr Distance operator""_km(long double val);
    friend constexpr Distance operator""_km(unsigned long long val);
    friend constexpr Distance operator""_mi(long double val);
    friend constexpr Distance operator""_mi(unsigned long long val);
    friend constexpr Distance operator""_ly(long double val);
    friend constexpr Distance operator""_ly(unsigned long long val);
};

// ===== User-defined literals =====

constexpr Distance operator""_m(long double val) {
    return Distance(static_cast<double>(val));
}
constexpr Distance operator""_m(unsigned long long val) {
    return Distance(static_cast<double>(val));
}

constexpr Distance operator""_km(long double val) {
    return Distance(static_cast<double>(val) * 1000.0);
}
constexpr Distance operator""_km(unsigned long long val) {
    return Distance(static_cast<double>(val) * 1000.0);
}

constexpr Distance operator""_mi(long double val) {
    return Distance(static_cast<double>(val) * 1609.344);
}
constexpr Distance operator""_mi(unsigned long long val) {
    return Distance(static_cast<double>(val) * 1609.344);
}

constexpr Distance operator""_ly(long double val) {
    return Distance(static_cast<double>(val) * 9.461e15);
}
constexpr Distance operator""_ly(unsigned long long val) {
    return Distance(static_cast<double>(val) * 9.461e15);
}

// ===== Compile-time verification =====

// All computation happens at compile time
static_assert(1_km == 1000_m);
static_assert((1_km).meters() == 1000.0);
static_assert(1_mi > 1_km);
static_assert((2_km + 500_m).meters() == 2500.0);
static_assert((10_km / 2_km) == 5.0);  // ratio

constexpr auto marathon = 42_km + 195_m;
static_assert(marathon.meters() == 42195.0);

int main() {
    // Natural, readable syntax
    auto height = 1.82_m;
    auto drive = 350.5_km;
    auto run = 26.2_mi;  // marathon in miles
    auto nearest_star = 4.24_ly;  // Proxima Centauri

    std::cout << "Height: " << height << "\n";
    std::cout << "Drive: " << drive << "\n";
    std::cout << "Marathon: " << run << " = " << run.kilometers() << " km\n";
    std::cout << "Nearest star: " << nearest_star << "\n";

    // Mixed-unit arithmetic works naturally
    auto total = 5_km + 3_mi;  // 5 km + 3 miles in meters
    std::cout << "\n5 km + 3 miles = " << total << "\n";

    // Comparisons
    std::cout << "1 mile > 1 km? " << (1_mi > 1_km ? "yes" : "no") << "\n";

    // Ratios
    std::cout << "Miles per km: " << 1_mi / 1_km << "\n";

    // Compile-time marathon distance
    constexpr auto m = 42_km + 195_m;
    std::cout << "\nMarathon: " << m << " = " << m.miles() << " miles\n";

    // Scale
    auto half_marathon = m / 2.0;
    std::cout << "Half marathon: " << half_marathon << "\n";

    return 0;
}
```
